# Getting Started [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html\#getting-started "Link to this heading")

Certain downloadable NIMs can be used on an RTX Windows system with Windows
Subsystem for Linux (WSL). To enable WSL version 2 on your system, follow the
steps on this page.

## Prerequisites [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html\#prerequisites "Link to this heading")

RTX 40-series and 50-series GeForce GPUs are supported. Some NIMs may require
specific hardware. Refer to
[NIM-specific documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/index.html) for
more information.

Windows 11 build 23H2 (and later) is supported. The system must have at least 12
GB of RAM.

## Installation [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html\#installation "Link to this heading")

You can use the [NVIDIA NIM WSL2 installer](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html#wsl2-installer) (recommended)
or [manually install the components](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html#wsl2-manual-install) yourself. The
installer automatically detects and installs the software dependencies needed to
enable GPU access in WSL2.

### Use the NVIDIA NIM WSL2 Installer (Recommended) [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html\#use-the-nvidia-nim-wsl2-installer-recommended "Link to this heading")

Follow these steps to install WSL2 with the NVIDIA AI Workbench included:

1. Make sure virtualization is enabled in the system BIOS. In Windows, open
Task Manager, select the **Performance** tab, and find **Virtualization**.
    ![Screenshot of virtualization enabled in Windows Task Manager](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/_images/virtualization-task-manager.png)
For more information, see
[Enable Virtualization on Windows](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/enable-virtualization-on-windows-c5578302-6e43-4b4b-a449-8ced115f58e1).

2. Install the latest
[NVIDIA Driver for Windows](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/drivers/). The
minimum supported driver version is 570.



Tip



Use the NVIDIA App to keep your drivers up to date and optimized.

3. Download the installer file.

[Download installer](https://assets.ngc.nvidia.com/products/api-catalog/rtx/NIMSetup.exe)

4. Unzip the downloaded file.

5. Run the executable setup file.

6. Follow the setup prompts. If this is the first installation of WSL2, then
restart the system to finish the installation.

7. [Verify the installation](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html#wsl2-verify-install).


### Manually Install Components (for Advanced Users) [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html\#manually-install-components-for-advanced-users "Link to this heading")

Follow these steps to manually install drivers and other software components for
WSL2:

01. Make sure virtualization is enabled in the system BIOS. In Windows, open
    Task Manager, select the **Performance** tab, and find **Virtualization**.
     ![Screenshot of virtualization enabled in Windows Task Manager](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/_images/virtualization-task-manager.png)
    For more information, see
    [Enable Virtualization on Windows](https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/enable-virtualization-on-windows-c5578302-6e43-4b4b-a449-8ced115f58e1).

02. Install the latest
    [NVIDIA Driver for Windows](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/drivers/). The
    minimum supported driver version is 570.



    Tip



    Use the NVIDIA App to keep your drivers up to date and optimized.

03. Install WSL2 Ubuntu on Windows. You can install the Linux distribution of
    your choice within WSL2; NVIDIA recommends Ubuntu 24.04 or later. Run the
    following command as administrator from Command Prompt or PowerShell:





    ```
    wsl --install --distribution Ubuntu-24.04
    ```

    Copy to clipboard



    If the `wsl --install` command fails, try the
    [manual installation steps for older versions of WSL](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install-manual#step-4---download-the-linux-kernel-update-package).

    For more information, see [Basic commands for WSL](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/basic-commands#install) and
    [How to install Linux on Windows with WSL](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install) .

04. Restart Windows to complete WSL2 installation.

05. Run the distribution as root to install other dependencies:





    ```
    wsl -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root
    ```

    Copy to clipboard

06. Install [Podman](https://podman.io/docs/installation#ubuntu):





    ```
    cd ~

    sudo apt-get update

    sudo apt-get -y install podman
    ```

    Copy to clipboard

07. Install `gcc` if it’s not already installed:





    ```
    sudo apt-get -y install gcc
    ```

    Copy to clipboard

08. Install [NVIDIA Container Toolkit](https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/cloud-native/container-toolkit/latest/install-guide.html#installing-with-apt):





    ```
    curl -fsSL https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/gpgkey | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg \
      && curl -s -L https://nvidia.github.io/libnvidia-container/stable/deb/nvidia-container-toolkit.list | \
        sed 's#deb https://#deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/nvidia-container-toolkit-keyring.gpg] https://#g' | \
        sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/nvidia-container-toolkit.list
    ```

    Copy to clipboard







    ```
    sudo apt-get update
    ```

    Copy to clipboard







    ```
    sudo apt-get install -y nvidia-container-toolkit
    ```

    Copy to clipboard

09. Restart Windows to initialize the drivers.

10. Run the distribution as root:





    ```
    wsl -d Ubuntu-24.04 -u root
    ```

    Copy to clipboard

11. Configure Podman by
    [generating a CDI specification](https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/cloud-native/container-toolkit/latest/cdi-support.html#generating-a-cdi-specification):





    ```
    sudo nvidia-ctk cdi generate --output=/etc/cdi/nvidia.yaml
    ```

    Copy to clipboard

12. [Verify the installation](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html#wsl2-verify-install).


## Verify the Installation [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html\#verify-the-installation "Link to this heading")

To verify that the installation succeeded, do the following:

1. Run WSL2 with the NVIDIA Workbench distribution:





```
wsl -d NVIDIA-Workbench
```

Copy to clipboard

2. Verify your container runtime supports NVIDIA GPUs by running the
following command:





```
podman run --rm --device=nvidia.com/gpu=all ubuntu nvidia-smi
```

Copy to clipboard



For a GeForce RTX 4090, the output should look like the following:





```
+----------------------------------------------------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+
| NVIDIA-SMI 570.86.16 Driver Version: 572.16 CUDA Version: 12.8 |                       |                       |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+
|| GPU Name Persistence-M                                        || Bus-Id Disp.A        || Volatile Uncorr. ECC |
|| Fan Temp Perf Pwr:Usage/Cap                                   || Memory-Usage         || GPU-Util Compute M.  |
||                                                               ||                      || MIG M.               |
|| 0 NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 On                                  || 00000000:2D:00.0 Off || Off                  |
|| 0% 36C P8 18W / 450W                                          || 349MiB / 24564MiB    || 0% Default           |
||                                                               ||                      || N/A                  |
+----------------------------------------------------------------+-----------------------+-----------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Processes:                                                                              |
|  GPU   GI   CI              PID   Type   Process name                        GPU Memory |
|        ID   ID                                                               Usage      |
|=========================================================================================|
|    0   N/A  N/A              23      G   /Xwayland                             N/A      |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

Copy to clipboard

3. Run simple performance tests on CUDA samples by running the following
command:





```
podman run --rm -it --device=nvidia.com/gpu=all  nvcr.io/nvidia/k8s/cuda-sample:nbody nbody -gpu -benchmark
```

Copy to clipboard



For a GeForce RTX 4090, the output should look like the following:





```
NOTE: The CUDA Samples are not meant for performance measurements. Results
may vary when GPU Boost is enabled.

> Windowed mode
> Simulation data stored in video memory
> Single precision floating point simulation
> 1 Devices used for simulation
MapSMtoCores for SM 8.9 is undefined.  Default to use 128 Cores/SM
MapSMtoArchName for SM 8.9 is undefined.  Default to use Ampere
GPU Device 0: "Ampere" with compute capability 8.9

> Compute 8.9 CUDA device: [NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090]
131072 bodies, total time for 10 iterations: 75.771 ms
= 2267.342 billion interactions per second
= 45346.835 single-precision GFLOP/s at 20 flops per interaction
```

Copy to clipboard

4. Verify that the NVIDIA Container Toolkit is installed by running the
following command:





```
nvidia-ctk --version
```

Copy to clipboard



The output should look like the following:





```
NVIDIA Container Toolkit CLI version 1.17.4
commit: 9b69590c7428470a72f2ae05f826412976af1395
```

Copy to clipboard


## Start a NIM [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/getting-started.html\#start-a-nim "Link to this heading")

Refer to [NIM-specific documentation](https://build.nvidia.com/search?q=run-on-rtx)
for instructions on how to start a NIM container.

# Troubleshooting [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#troubleshooting "Link to this heading")

## Installation [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#installation "Link to this heading")

### Installer fails [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#installer-fails "Link to this heading")

Installation failures can be caused by the following:

- Windows 11 has pending required updates.

Run Windows Update and install all required updates. Then try running the
installer again.

- You are being rate limited by GitHub.

WSL is downloaded and updated directly from GitHub. If you are on a shared
network where users access GitHub with unregistered accounts, you may run into
a rate limiter. To verify, run the following command and check that there are
remaining credits in the `core` section of the output:





```
curl https://api.github.com/rate_limit
```

Copy to clipboard







```
"resources": {
    "core": {
      "limit": 60,
      "remaining": 0,
      "reset": 1741626149,
      "used": 1,
      "resource": "core"
    },
```

Copy to clipboard



If there are no credits remaining, you need to wait and try again after about
one hour or relocate the computer to a non-shared network with remaining
GitHub API credits.


### Installer freezes or hangs for an indefinite amount of time [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#installer-freezes-or-hangs-for-an-indefinite-amount-of-time "Link to this heading")

1. Check if the installer is frozen due to one of the following reasons:

   - Checking for an internet connection

   - Updating software components inside the WSL2 distribution

   - Showing an unhandled exception message on the installer screen
2. Kill the installer process from Task Manager.

3. Reboot the system and run the installer again.


### Installer fails to configure WSL2 or fails to import the distribution [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#installer-fails-to-configure-wsl2-or-fails-to-import-the-distribution "Link to this heading")

1. Check for pending Windows updates and reboot your computer if necessary.

2. Run `wsl --update manually` in Windows PowerShell. If your internet
connection is unstable, you may need to run this command multiple times.

3. If you have an old version of WSL,
[manually install WSL2](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install).

4. Reboot the system and run the installer again.


### Installer fails to configure the distribution [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#installer-fails-to-configure-the-distribution "Link to this heading")

1. Run `wsl -l` in Command Prompt and check if the NVIDIA-Workbench
distribution is listed.

2. Run `wsl  --update` in Command Prompt and make sure WSL2 is updated to the
latest version

3. Run `wsl  --unregister NVIDIA-Workbench` in Command Prompt to unregister
the distribution.

4. Reboot the system and run the installer again.


### I can’t find the installation logs [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#i-can-t-find-the-installation-logs "Link to this heading")

By default, installation logs are created in the `%temp%/NvidiaLogging`
directory. Installation logs are temporary and are deleted when the installer
process exits.

To create persistent logs in a specified directory, run the installer with the
following command line options:

```
<installer-filename> -log:<your-log-directory> -loglevel:6
```

Copy to clipboard

For example: `setup.exe -log:C:\logs -loglevel:6`

## WSL2 configuration [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#wsl2-configuration "Link to this heading")

### I need to change a WSL2 installation [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#i-need-to-change-a-wsl2-installation "Link to this heading")

You can set a WSL2 configuration in the following ways:

- Edit the `.wslconfig` file for all WSL2 distributions, per user. Locate this
file in the `C:\Users\<username>` directory. If the file is not present in
this location, create a new text file named `.wslconfig` to customize all of
your WSL2 installations.

- Edit the `/etc/wsl.config` file for distribution-specific settings.


For more information, see the
[Microsoft documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/wsl-config#what-is-the-difference-between-wslconf-and-wslconfig).

## System RAM and disk space [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#system-ram-and-disk-space "Link to this heading")

### I need to modify the amount of system RAM available for WSL2 use [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#i-need-to-modify-the-amount-of-system-ram-available-for-wsl2-use "Link to this heading")

Add the following to the [WSL2 configuration](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html#wsl2-change-installation)
(`.wslconfig` file):

```
# Settings apply across all Linux distros running on WSL 2.
[wsl2]

# Limits VM memory to use no more than 32 GB. This can be set as whole numbers
# using GB or MB. On windows, the default is 50% of available SRAM.
memory = 32GB
```

Copy to clipboard

This allows the WSL2 instance to utilize up to the specified memory size.

### WSL2 is not able to use more than 50% of available SRAM [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#wsl2-is-not-able-to-use-more-than-50-of-available-sram "Link to this heading")

By default, WSL2 uses 50% of available SRAM. This limit can be changed by adding
the following configuration in the `.wslconfig` file:

```
# Settings apply across all Linux distros running on WSL 2.
[wsl2]

# Limits VM memory to use no more than 32 GB. This can be set as whole numbers
# using GB or MB. On windows, the default is 50% of available SRAM.
memory = 32GB
```

Copy to clipboard

For more information, see
[Advanced settings configuration in WSL](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/wsl-config#what-is-the-difference-between-wslconf-and-wslconfig).

### Upon loading a NIM, the WSL2 instance uses too much memory [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#upon-loading-a-nim-the-wsl2-instance-uses-too-much-memory "Link to this heading")

Use any of the following options to reduce system memory utilization inside WSL2.
These commands require `sudo` access to the distributions.

- Set `"autoMemoryReclaim=dropcache"` in the WSL2 configuration. This setting
automatically releases cached memory after detecting idle CPU usage. Set to
`gradual` for slow release; it waits for around 5 minutes with no CPU
activity and then releases the caches. Set to `dropcache` to instantly
release cached memory.

- If you need to explicitly clear caches within the WSL2 distribution, run the
following command in PowerShell:





```
wsl -d NVIDIA-Workbench -u root
echo 1 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches
// NVIDIA-Workbench is the distribution name from NIM Installer
```

Copy to clipboard

- Run the following command with `sudo` privileges within the WSL2
distribution:





```
sudo sh -c 'echo 1 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches'`
```

Copy to clipboard


### Disk space isn’t restored after I uninstall a NIM [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#disk-space-isn-t-restored-after-i-uninstall-a-nim "Link to this heading")

WSL2 doesn’t automatically release file storage, even after deleting the files
from the distribution. Therefore, `vhdx` size on disk keeps increasing. After
setting `sparseVhd=true`, any new VHD is set to sparse automatically. To
reduce the size of existing VHDs, use the DiskPart utility:

```
diskpart
select vdisk file="<path-to-vhdx>"
compact vdisk
exit
```

Copy to clipboard

## Errors and timeouts [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#errors-and-timeouts "Link to this heading")

### TLS issues within WSL2 [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html\#tls-issues-within-wsl2 "Link to this heading")

TLS-related timeouts and errors could be related to the MTU size. For more
information, see a
[related GitHub issue](https://github.com/microsoft/WSL/issues/4698#issuecomment-871279847).
You can try running the following commands:

```
sudo ip link set dev eth0 mtu 1350
ip link list
```

Copy to clipboard

# NVIDIA Speech NIM Microservices Overview [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/about/index.html\#nvidia-speech-nim-microservices-overview "Link to this heading")

NVIDIA Speech NIM microservices are GPU-accelerated Docker containers that provide speech AI capabilities as building blocks for your applications. Each NIM microservice packages a Nemotron model, the full NVIDIA inference stack (CUDA, TensorRT, Triton), and a unified API into a single container that you deploy, scale, and interact with through standard gRPC and HTTP interfaces.

You do not interact with models directly. Instead, each NIM microservice provides the API layer that your application calls to run inference on the containerized models.

## NIM Microservices [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/about/index.html\#nim-microservices "Link to this heading")

Each NIM microservice serves a Nemotron model and exposes it through a dedicated API.

| NIM Microservice | Supported Model Family | Input | Output | Default API Protocols |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **NVIDIA ASR NIM** | Nemotron ASR | Audio stream or audio buffer | Text transcripts with optional metadata | gRPC (port 50051), HTTP (port 9000) |
| **NVIDIA TTS NIM** | Nemotron TTS | Text | Synthesized speech audio | gRPC (port 50051), HTTP (port 9000) |
| **NVIDIA NMT NIM** | Nemotron NMT | Text (source language) | Text (target language) | gRPC (port 50051), HTTP (port 9000) |

You can deploy only the NIM microservices your application needs. Each runs as an independent Docker container with GPU acceleration. You select a specific model at deploy time by setting the `CONTAINER_ID` and `NIM_TAGS_SELECTOR` environment variables. For supported models and container IDs, refer to the [Support Matrix](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/reference/support-matrix/index.html).

To try each NIM microservice, visit [build.nvidia.com](https://build.nvidia.com/explore/speech).

## Building Applications with Speech NIM Microservices [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/about/index.html\#building-applications-with-speech-nim-microservices "Link to this heading")

Speech NIM microservices are building blocks. Your application sends requests to the NIM container APIs and receives results. The NIM handles model loading, GPU execution, batching, and streaming internally.

gRPC / HTTP

gRPC / HTTP

gRPC / HTTP

Your Application

ASR NIM

TTS NIM

NMT NIM

You can chain multiple NIM microservices together for complex pipelines. For example, a real-time translation application calls the ASR NIM to transcribe audio, passes the transcript to the NMT NIM for translation, and sends the translated text to the TTS NIM for speech synthesis. By integrating the NVIDIA Speech NIM microservices, you can orchestrate the data flow and build complex pipelines for end-to-end speech applications while scaling each NIM microservice independently.

## Use Cases [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/about/index.html\#use-cases "Link to this heading")

| Use Case | NIM Microservices | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Call center transcription | ASR NIM | Transcribe customer calls in real time or batch for analytics, compliance, and agent assistance. |
| Voice-enabled applications | ASR NIM + TTS NIM | Add speech input and spoken responses to virtual assistants, kiosks, or IoT devices. |
| Multilingual customer support | ASR NIM + NMT NIM | Transcribe speech and translate to an agent’s language for cross-language support workflows. |
| Real-time translation pipeline | ASR NIM + NMT NIM + TTS NIM | Capture speech, translate the transcript, and synthesize the output in the target language for live interpretation. |
| Accessibility and captioning | ASR NIM | Generate real-time captions or subtitles for live events, meetings, and media. |
| Content localization | NMT NIM + TTS NIM | Translate text content and produce localized voice-overs for training materials, videos, or documentation. |

## Next Steps [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/about/index.html\#next-steps "Link to this heading")

- [How It Works](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/about/how-it-works.html): Learn how the NVIDIA Speech NIM microservices work together to build speech applications.

- [Release Notes](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/about/release-notes.html): Track the release notes for the NVIDIA Speech NIM microservices.

- [Support Matrix](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/reference/support-matrix/index.html): Find supported models and hardware requirements.

- [Get Started](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/index.html): Learn how to deploy Speech NIM microservices with Docker.

# Run NVIDIA Speech NIM Microservices on Windows with WSL2 [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#run-nvidia-speech-nim-microservices-on-windows-with-wsl2 "Link to this heading")

This guide shows how to run NVIDIA Speech NIM microservices on Windows with WSL2. You will learn how to configure WSL2 for NIM containers and how to set up port forwarding when the client runs on a different machine than the WSL2 host.

Unlike the other tutorials in this section, this page is environment-specific. It covers the same deployment workflow (start a container, check readiness, send requests) but adapted for the WSL2 environment. Complete at least the [ASR tutorial](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/asr.html) first to learn the core workflow on a standard Linux system.

## What You Learn [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#what-you-learn "Link to this heading")

By completing this guide, you:

- Configure WSL2 memory limits for NIM containers.

- Deploy ASR and TTS NIMs on WSL2 using the same container workflow.

- Set up Windows port forwarding so clients on other machines can reach the NIM running in WSL2.

- Configure Windows Firewall rules for NIM ports.


## Prerequisites [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#prerequisites "Link to this heading")

Note

Support for NIMs on WSL is currently in Public Beta.

Before starting, complete the following:

1. Follow [NVIDIA NIM on WSL2](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/) for WSL2 installation and GPU passthrough configuration.

2. Check the [support matrix](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/reference/support-matrix/index.html) to confirm your GPU and model are supported on WSL2. Not all models are supported in this environment.

3. Configure WSL memory. By default, WSL can use up to half of system RAM. NIM containers need significant memory. To adjust the limit, create a `.wslconfig` file in your Windows user profile directory (`C:\Users\<UserName>`):





```
[wsl2]
memory=38GB
```

Copy to clipboard



Then restart WSL:





```
wsl --shutdown
```

Copy to clipboard



If WSL memory is too low, the NIM container will fail during model loading or engine building. Set this to at least 32 GB for most models.

For WSL issues, refer to [WSL configuration and troubleshooting](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/wsl2/latest/troubleshooting.html).

4. On WSL2 you can need to use `podman` instead of `docker`. Substitute `podman` for `docker` in the commands below if your environment requires it.

5. Complete [NGC access setup](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/ngc-access-setup.html) and run `docker login nvcr.io` (or equivalent for podman).


## Same-Machine Setup (Client Inside WSL2) [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#same-machine-setup-client-inside-wsl2 "Link to this heading")

If both the NIM container and your client run inside the same WSL2 environment, the workflow is identical to standard Linux. The only difference is model selection: choose smaller profiles that fit within WSL2 memory constraints.

### Step 1: Launch the NIM [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#step-1-launch-the-nim "Link to this heading")

Open a WSL2 terminal and deploy your service.

ASR

Deploy the Parakeet 0.6b en-US **streaming** profile. This is a smaller model suited to WSL2 memory constraints.

```
export CONTAINER_ID=parakeet-0-6b-ctc-en-us
export NIM_TAGS_SELECTOR="name=parakeet-0-6b-ctc-en-us,mode=str,bs=1"

docker run -it --rm --name=$CONTAINER_ID \
  --runtime=nvidia \
  --gpus '"device=0"' \
  --shm-size=8GB \
  -e NGC_API_KEY \
  -e NIM_HTTP_API_PORT=9000 \
  -e NIM_GRPC_API_PORT=50051 \
  -p 9000:9000 \
  -p 50051:50051 \
  -e NIM_TAGS_SELECTOR \
  nvcr.io/nim/nvidia/$CONTAINER_ID:latest
```

Copy to clipboard

The `NIM_TAGS_SELECTOR` specifies `bs=1` (batch size 1), which reduces memory usage. On WSL2, prefer the smallest batch size and a single inference mode (`mode=str` or `mode=offline`).

For other ASR models and modes, refer to the [ASR tutorial](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/asr.html) and the [support matrix](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/reference/support-matrix/index.html).

TTS

Deploy Magpie TTS Flow (or another WSL-supported TTS model).

```
export CONTAINER_ID=magpie-tts-flow
export NIM_TAGS_SELECTOR=name=magpie-tts-flow

docker run -it --rm --name=$CONTAINER_ID \
  --runtime=nvidia \
  --gpus '"device=0"' \
  --shm-size=8GB \
  -e NGC_API_KEY \
  -e NIM_HTTP_API_PORT=9000 \
  -e NIM_GRPC_API_PORT=50051 \
  -p 9000:9000 \
  -p 50051:50051 \
  -e NIM_TAGS_SELECTOR \
  nvcr.io/nim/nvidia/$CONTAINER_ID:latest
```

Copy to clipboard

For other TTS models, refer to the [TTS tutorial](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/tts.html) and the [support matrix](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/reference/support-matrix/index.html). Not all TTS models are supported on WSL.

### Step 2: Run Client Inference [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#step-2-run-client-inference "Link to this heading")

When the NIM is ready (`/v1/health/ready` returns `{"status":"ready"}`), open a **new** WSL2 terminal and follow the inference steps from the corresponding tutorial:

ASR

Follow [Step 5: Streaming Transcription](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/asr.html#asr-tutorial-step-5-streaming-transcription) in the ASR tutorial.

Note

With the **streaming** profile above, only **streaming** client examples work. For **offline** transcription, deploy a profile with `mode=offline`. See the [support matrix](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/reference/support-matrix/index.html).

TTS

Follow [Step 4: Synthesize Speech](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/tts.html#step-4-synthesize-speech) in the TTS tutorial.

## Remote Client Setup (Client on a Different Machine) [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#remote-client-setup-client-on-a-different-machine "Link to this heading")

If the client runs on a different machine than the WSL2 host, you need port forwarding because WSL2 uses NAT networking. The NIM binds to ports inside WSL2, but those ports are not directly accessible from the external network.

Use a Windows terminal with **Administrator** rights for the following steps.

### Step 1: Get the WSL2 IP [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#step-1-get-the-wsl2-ip "Link to this heading")

In the WSL2 terminal:

```
hostname -I
```

Copy to clipboard

Use the first address (for example, `172.x.x.x`) as `<wsl2-ip>` in the next steps.

WSL2 gets a private IP on a virtual network. Windows port forwarding maps the external Windows IP to this internal WSL2 IP.

### Step 2: Forward Ports from Windows to WSL2 [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#step-2-forward-ports-from-windows-to-wsl2 "Link to this heading")

In an elevated Windows command prompt or PowerShell, create port forwarding rules for both NIM ports.

Forward HTTP port 9000:

```
netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 listenport=9000 listenaddress=0.0.0.0 connectport=9000 connectaddress=<wsl2-ip>
```

Copy to clipboard

Forward gRPC port 50051:

```
netsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 listenport=50051 listenaddress=0.0.0.0 connectport=50051 connectaddress=<wsl2-ip>
```

Copy to clipboard

Replace `<wsl2-ip>` with the value from Step 1.

Note

WSL2’s IP can change after each restart. Update the portproxy rules with the new IP when you restart WSL2.

### Step 3: Verify Port Forwarding [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#step-3-verify-port-forwarding "Link to this heading")

```
netsh interface portproxy show v4tov4
```

Copy to clipboard

You should refer to entries for ports 9000 and 50051 connecting to `<wsl2-ip>`.

### Step 4: Allow Traffic Through Windows Firewall [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#step-4-allow-traffic-through-windows-firewall "Link to this heading")

In PowerShell **as Administrator**:

```
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Allow NIM HTTP on Port 9000" -Direction Inbound -Action Allow -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 9000
New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName "Allow NIM gRPC on Port 50051" -Direction Inbound -Action Allow -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 50051
```

Copy to clipboard

These commands create inbound firewall rules that allow external traffic on the NIM ports. Without these rules, the Windows firewall blocks incoming connections even though port forwarding is configured.

### Step 5: Run the Client from the Remote Machine [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#step-5-run-the-client-from-the-remote-machine "Link to this heading")

On the client machine, use the **Windows host’s IP** (not the WSL2 IP) in all commands. Replace `<windows-host-ip>` with the Windows machine’s network IP.

Verify the service is reachable from the remote machine:

```
curl -X 'GET' 'http://<windows-host-ip>:9000/v1/health/ready'
```

Copy to clipboard

Run inference using the examples for your service:

ASR

```
python3 python-clients/scripts/asr/transcribe_file.py \
  --server <windows-host-ip>:50051 \
  --language-code en-US --automatic-punctuation \
  --input-file en-US_sample.wav
```

Copy to clipboard

TTS

```
python3 python-clients/scripts/tts/talk.py --server <windows-host-ip>:50051 \
  --language-code en-US \
  --text "Hello from the client machine." \
  --voice Magpie-Multilingual.EN-US.Aria \
  --output output.wav
```

Copy to clipboard

## What You Learned [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/speech/latest/get-started/tutorials/wsl.html\#what-you-learned "Link to this heading")

In this guide, you have learned the following:

- Configured WSL2 memory to support NIM containers, understanding that insufficient memory causes model loading failures.

- Selected smaller model profiles (`bs=1`, single inference mode) appropriate for the WSL2 environment.

- Set up port forwarding from Windows to WSL2, understanding that WSL2’s NAT networking requires explicit forwarding for external access.

- Configured firewall rules to allow external traffic on NIM ports.

- Verified the complete path from a remote client through Windows port forwarding to the NIM running inside WSL2.

# Getting Started [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#getting-started "Link to this heading")

## Prerequisites [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#prerequisites "Link to this heading")

### Setup [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#setup "Link to this heading")

1. **NVIDIA AI Enterprise License**: NVIDIA NIM for LLMs are available for self-hosting under the NVIDIA AI Enterprise (NVAIE) License. Sign up for NVAIE license [here](https://build.nvidia.com/meta/llama-3-8b-instruct?snippet_tab=Docker&signin=true&integrate_nim=true&self_hosted_api=true)

2. **NVIDIA GPU(s)**: NVIDIA NIM for LLMs (NIM for LLMs) runs on any NVIDIA GPU with sufficient GPU memory, but some model/GPU combinations are optimized. Homogeneous multi-GPUs systems with [tensor parallelism](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/what-is-nvidia-nvlink/) enabled are also supported. See the [Support Matrix](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/support-matrix.html) for more information.

3. **CPU**: x86\_64 architecture only for this release

4. **OS**: any Linux distributions which:

   - Are [supported by the NVIDIA Container toolkit](https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/cloud-native/container-toolkit/latest/supported-platforms.html)

   - Have `glibc` >= 2.35 (see output of `ld -v`)
5. **CUDA Drivers**: Follow the [installation guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-installation-guide-linux). We recommend:

   - Using a network repository as part of a [package manager installation](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-installation-guide-linux/#package-manager-installation), skipping the CUDA toolkit installation as the libraries are available within the NIM container, then

   - Installing the [open kernels](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-installation-guide-linux/#nvidia-open-gpu-kernel-modules) for a [specific version](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-installation-guide-linux/#driver-installation):




     | Major Version | EOL | Data Center & RTX/Quadro GPUs | GeForce GPUs |
     | --- | --- | --- | --- |
     | `> 550` | _TBD_ | X | X |
     | `550` | Feb. 2025 | X | X |
     | `545` | Oct. 2023 | X | X |
     | `535` | June 2026 | X |  |
     | `525` | Nov. 2023 | X |  |
     | `470` | Sept. 2024 | X |  |
6. Install [Docker](https://docs.docker.com/engine/install/)

7. Install the [NVIDIA Container Toolkit](https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/cloud-native/container-toolkit/latest/install-guide.html#installing-the-nvidia-container-toolkit)


Note

After installing the toolkit, follow the instructions in the [Configure Docker](https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/cloud-native/container-toolkit/latest/install-guide.html#configuring-docker) section in the NVIDIA Container Toolkit documentation.

* * *

To ensure that your setup is correct, run the following command:

```
docker run --rm --runtime=nvidia --gpus all ubuntu nvidia-smi
```

Copy to clipboard

This command should produce output similar to one of the following, where you can confirm CUDA driver version, and available GPUs.

```

```

Copy to clipboard

#### Installing WSL2 for Windows [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#installing-wsl2-for-windows "Link to this heading")

Certain downloadable NIMs can be used on an RTX Windows system with Windows System for Linux (WSL).
To enable WSL2, perform the following steps.

1. Be sure your computer is capable of running WSL2 as described in the [Prerequisites](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install#prerequisites) section of the WSL2 documentation.

2. Enable WSL2 on your Windows computer by following the steps listed in [Install WSL command](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install#install-wsl-command). By default these steps install the Ubuntu distribution of Linux.
For a list of alternative installations, see [Change the default Linux distribution installed](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install#change-the-default-linux-distribution-installed).


## Launch NVIDIA NIM for LLMs [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#launch-nvidia-nim-for-llms "Link to this heading")

You can download and run the NIM of your choice from either the API catalog or the NGC.

### Option 1: From API Catalog [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#option-1-from-api-catalog "Link to this heading")

Checkout this [video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=087spL8hMvM), which illustrates the following steps.

#### Generate an API Key [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#generate-an-api-key "Link to this heading")

1. Navigate to the LLM you wish to deploy from the [API Catalog](https://build.nvidia.com/).

2. Select “Docker” in the upper right pane.

![Click Docker](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/_images/build_docker_tab.png)

3. Select “Get API Key” and login if prompted.

![Get API Key](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/_images/build_get_api_key.png)

4. Select “Generate Key”

![Generate Key](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/_images/build_generate_key.png)

5. Copy your key and store it in a secure place. Don’t share it.

![Copy Key](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/_images/build_copy_key.png)


#### Login to Docker [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#login-to-docker "Link to this heading")

Use the `docker login` command, as shown in the following screenshot, to log in to Docker. Replace the placeholders for Username and Password with your values.

![Docker login](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/_images/build_docker_login.png)

#### Download and Launch NVIDIA NIM for LLMs [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#download-and-launch-nvidia-nim-for-llms "Link to this heading")

Use the following command to pull and run the NIM using Docker.

![Docker Run](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/_images/build_docker_run.png)

To modify the docker run parameters, see [Docker Run Parameters](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html#docker-run-parameters).

Now, you can jump to [running inference](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html#run-inference).

### Option 2: From NGC [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#option-2-from-ngc "Link to this heading")

#### Generate an API key [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#id1 "Link to this heading")

An NGC API key is required to access NGC resources and a key can be generated here: [https://org.ngc.nvidia.com/setup/personal-keys](https://org.ngc.nvidia.com/setup/personal-keys).

When creating an NGC API key, ensure that at least “NGC Catalog” is selected from the “Services Included” dropdown. More Services can be included if this key is to be reused for other purposes.

![Generate Personal Key](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/_images/personal-key.png)

#### Export the API key [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#export-the-api-key "Link to this heading")

Pass the value of the API key to the `docker run` command in the next section as the `NGC_CLI_API_KEY` environment variable to download the appropriate models and resources when starting the NIM.

If you’re not familiar with how to create the `NGC_CLI_API_KEY` environment variable, the simplest way is to export it in your terminal:

```
export NGC_CLI_API_KEY=<value>
```

Copy to clipboard

Run one of the following commands to make the key available at startup:

```
# If using bash
echo "export NGC_CLI_API_KEY=<value>" >> ~/.bashrc

# If using zsh
echo "export NGC_CLI_API_KEY=<value>" >> ~/.zshrc
```

Copy to clipboard

Note

Other, more secure options include saving the value in a file, so that you can retrieve with `cat $NGC_CLI_API_KEY_FILE`, or using a [password manager](https://www.passwordstore.org/).

#### Docker Login to NGC [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#docker-login-to-ngc "Link to this heading")

To pull the NIM container image from NGC, first authenticate with the NVIDIA Container Registry with the following command:

```
echo "$NGC_CLI_API_KEY" | docker login nvcr.io --username '$oauthtoken' --password-stdin
```

Copy to clipboard

Use `$oauthtoken` as the username and `NGC_CLI_API_KEY` as the password. The `$oauthtoken` username is a special name that indicates that you will authenticate with an API key and not a user name and password.

#### List Available NIMs [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#list-available-nims "Link to this heading")

This documentation uses the `ngc` CLI tool in a number of examples.
See the [NGC CLI documentation](https://docs.ngc.nvidia.com/cli/index.html)
for information on downloading and configure the tool.

Use the following command to list the available NIMs, in CSV format.

```
ngc registry image list --format_type csv nvcr.io/nim/*
```

Copy to clipboard

This command should produce output in the following format:

```
Name,Repository,Latest Tag,Image Size,Updated Date,Permission,Signed Tag?,Access Type,Associated Products
<name1>,<repository1>,<latest tag1>,<image size1>,<updated date1>,<permission1>,<signed tag?1>,<access type1>,<associated products1>
...
<nameN>,<repositoryN>,<latest tagN>,<image sizeN>,<updated dateN>,<permissionN>,<signed tag?N>,<access typeN>,<associated productsN>
```

Copy to clipboard

Use the **Repository** and **Latest Tag** fields when you call the `docker run` command, as shown in the following section.

#### Launch NIM [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#launch-nim "Link to this heading")

The following command launches a Docker container for the `llama3-8b-instruct` model. To launch a container for a different NIM, replace the values of `Repository` and `Latest_Tag` with values from the previous `image list` command and change the value of `CONTAINER_NAME` to something appropriate.

You can tell that you have the correct `Repository` and `Latest_Tag` values by getting information about the model with the following command:

```
ngc registry model info --format_type ascii ${Repository}:${Latest_Tag}
```

Copy to clipboard

Which should produce output like the following:

```
----------------------------------------------------------
 Model Version Information
   Id: 0.10.0+e6f46027-h100x1-fp16-balanced.24.06.15839955
   Batch Size:
   Memory Footprint:
   Number Of Epochs:
   Accuracy Reached:
   GPU Model:
   Access Type:
   Associated Products:
   Created Date: 2024-06-14T22:28:17.604Z
   Description:
   Status: UPLOAD_COMPLETE
   Total File Count: 11
   Total Size: 14.96 GB
----------------------------------------------------------
```

Copy to clipboard

```
# Choose a container name for bookkeeping
export CONTAINER_NAME=Llama3-8B-Instruct

# The container name from the previous ngc registgry image list command
Repository=nim/meta/llama3-8b-instruct
Latest_Tag=1.0.0

# Choose a LLM NIM Image from NGC
export IMG_NAME="${__ngc_container_root}/${Repository}:${Latest_Tag}"

# Choose a path on your system to cache the downloaded models
export LOCAL_NIM_CACHE=~/.cache/nim
mkdir -p "$LOCAL_NIM_CACHE"

# Start the LLM NIM
docker run -it --rm --name=$CONTAINER_NAME \
  --runtime=nvidia \
  --gpus all \
  --shm-size=16GB \
  -e NGC_CLI_API_KEY=$NGC_CLI_API_KEY \
  -v "$LOCAL_NIM_CACHE:/opt/nim/.cache" \
  -u $(id -u) \
  -p 8000:8000 \
  $IMG_NAME
```

Copy to clipboard

## Docker Run Parameters [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#docker-run-parameters "Link to this heading")

| Flags | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `-it` | `--interactive` \+ `--tty` (see Docker docs) |
| `--rm` | Delete the container after it stops (see Docker docs) |
| `--name=llama3-8b-instruct` | Give a name to the NIM container for bookkeeping (here `llama3-8b-instruct`). Use any preferred value. |
| `--runtime=nvidia` | Ensure NVIDIA drivers are accessible in the container. |
| `--gpus all` | Expose all NVIDIA GPUs inside the container. See the [configuration page](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/configuration.html) for mounting specific GPUs. |
| `--shm-size=16GB` | Allocate host memory for multi-GPU communication. Not required for single GPU models or GPUs with NVLink enabled. |
| `-e NGC_CLI_API_KEY` | Provide the container with the token necessary to download adequate models and resources from NGC. See [Export the API key](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html#export-the-api-key). |
| `-v "$LOCAL_NIM_CACHE:/opt/nim/.cache"` | Mount a cache directory from your system (`~/.cache/nim` here) inside the NIM (defaults to `/opt/nim/.cache`), allowing downloaded models and artifacts to be reused by follow-up runs. |
| `-u $(id -u)` | Use the same user as your system user inside the NIM container to avoid permission mismatches when downloading models in your local cache directory. |
| `-p 8000:8000` | Forward the port where the NIM server is published inside the container to access from the host system. The left-hand side of `:` is the host system ip:port (`8000` here), while the right-hand side is the container port where the NIM server is published (defaults to `8000`). |
| `$IMG_NAME` | Name and version of the LLM NIM container from NGC. The LLM NIM server automatically starts if no argument is provided after this. |

Note

See the [Configuring a NIM](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/configuration.html) topic for information about additional configuration settings.

Note

If you have an issue with permission mismatches when downloading models in your local cache directory, add the `-u $(id -u)` option to the `docker run` call.

* * *

Note

NIM automatically selects the most suitable profile based on your system specification. For details, see [Automatic Profile Selection](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/optimization.html#automatic-profile-selection)

## Run Inference [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#run-inference "Link to this heading")

During startup the NIM container downloads the required resources and begins serving the model behind an API endpoint. The following message indicates a successful startup.

```
INFO: Application startup complete.
INFO: Uvicorn running on http://0.0.0.0:8000 (Press CTRL+C to quit)
```

Copy to clipboard

Once you see this message you can validate the deployment of NIM by executing an inference request. In a new terminal, run the following command to show a list of models available for inference:

```
curl -X GET 'http://0.0.0.0:8000/v1/models'
```

Copy to clipboard

Tip

Pipe the results of `curl` commands into a tool like [`jq`](https://jqlang.github.io/jq/) or `python -m json.tool` to make the output of the API easier to read.
For example: `curl -s http://0.0.0.0:8000/v1/models | jq`.

This command should produce output similar to the following:

```
{
   "object": "list",
   "data": [\
     {\
       "id": "meta/llama3-8b-instruct",\
       "object": "model",\
       "created": 1715659875,\
       "owned_by": "vllm",\
       "root": "meta/llama3-8b-instruct",\
       "parent": null,\
       "permission": [\
         {\
           "id": "modelperm-e39aaffe7015444eba964fa7736ae653",\
           "object": "model_permission",\
           "created": 1715659875,\
           "allow_create_engine": false,\
           "allow_sampling": true,\
           "allow_logprobs": true,\
           "allow_search_indices": false,\
           "allow_view": true,\
           "allow_fine_tuning": false,\
           "organization": "*",\
           "group": null,\
           "is_blocking": false\
         }\
       ]\
     }\
   ]
 }
```

Copy to clipboard

### OpenAI Completion Request [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#openai-completion-request "Link to this heading")

The Completions endpoint is typically used for `base` models. With the Completions endpoint, prompts are sent as plain strings, and the model produces the most likely text completions subject to the other parameters chosen. To stream the result, set `"stream": true`.

Important

Update the model name to suit your requirements. For example, for a `llama3-8b-instruct` model,
you might use the following command:

```
curl -X 'POST' \
    'http://0.0.0.0:8000/v1/completions' \
    -H 'accept: application/json' \
    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    -d '{
      "model": "meta/llama3-8b-instruct",
      "prompt": "Once upon a time",
      "max_tokens": 64
    }'
```

Copy to clipboard

You can also use the [OpenAI Python API library](https://github.com/openai/openai-python).

```
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(base_url="http://0.0.0.0:8000/v1", api_key="not-used")
prompt = "Once upon a time"
response = client.completions.create(
    model="meta/llama3-8b-instruct",
    prompt=prompt,
    max_tokens=16,
    stream=False
)
completion = response.choices[0].text
print(completion)

# Prints:
# , there was a young man named Jack who lived in a small village at the
```

Copy to clipboard

### OpenAI Chat Completion Request [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#openai-chat-completion-request "Link to this heading")

The Chat Completions endpoint is typically used with `chat` or `instruct` tuned models that are designed to be used through a conversational approach. With the Chat Completions endpoint, prompts are sent in the form of messages with roles and contents, giving a natural way to keep track of a multi-turn conversation. To stream the result, set `"stream": true`.

Important

Update model name according to your requirements. For example, for a `llama3-8b-instruct` model, you might use the following command:

```
curl -X 'POST' \
'http://0.0.0.0:8000/v1/chat/completions' \
    -H 'accept: application/json' \
    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    -d '{
      "model": "meta/llama3-8b-instruct",
      "messages": [\
          {\
              "role":"user",\
              "content":"Hello! How are you?"\
          },\
          {\
              "role":"assistant",\
              "content":"Hi! I am quite well, how can I help you today?"\
          },\
          {\
              "role":"user",\
              "content":"Can you write me a song?"\
          }\
      ],
      "max_tokens": 32
    }'
```

Copy to clipboard

You can also use the [OpenAI Python API library](https://github.com/openai/openai-python).

```
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI(base_url="http://0.0.0.0:8000/v1", api_key="not-used")
messages = [\
    {"role": "user", "content": "Hello! How are you?"},\
    {"role": "assistant", "content": "Hi! I am quite well, how can I help you today?"},\
    {"role": "user", "content": "Write a short limerick about the wonders of GPU computing."}\
]
chat_response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="meta/llama3-8b-instruct",
    messages=messages,
    max_tokens=32,
    stream=False
)
assistant_message = chat_response.choices[0].message
print(assistant_message)

# Prints:
# ChatCompletionMessage(content='There once was a GPU so fine,\nProcessed data in parallel so divine,\nIt crunched with great zest,\nAnd computational quest,\nUnleashing speed, a true wonder sublime!', role='assistant', function_call=None, tool_calls=None)
```

Copy to clipboard

Attention

If you encounter a `BadRequestError` with an error message indicating that you are missing the `messages` or `prompt` field, you might inadvertently be using the wrong endpoint.

For example, if you make a Completions request with a request body intended for Chat Completions, you get the following error:

```
{
  "object": "error",
  "message": "[{'type': 'missing', 'loc': ('body', 'prompt'), 'msg': 'Field required', ...",\
  "type": "BadRequestError",\
  "param": null,\
  "code": 400\
}\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
Conversely, if you make a Chat Completions request with a request body intended for Completions, you get the following error:\
\
```\
{\
  "object": "error",\
  "message": "[{'type': 'missing', 'loc': ('body', 'messages'), 'msg': 'Field required', ...",\
  "type": "BadRequestError",\
  "param": null,\
  "code": 400\
}\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
Verify that the endpoint you are using, such as `/v1/completions` or `/v1/chat/completions`, is correctly configured for your request.\
\
### Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#parameter-efficient-fine-tuning "Link to this heading")\
\
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods enable efficient adaptation of large pre-trained models. Currently NIM only supports LoRA PEFT.\
See [Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/peft.html) for details.\
\
## Stopping the container [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#stopping-the-container "Link to this heading")\
\
If a Docker container is launched with the `--name` command line option, you can use the following command to stop the running container.\
\
```\
docker stop $CONTAINER_NAME\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
Use `docker kill` if `stop` isn’t responsive. Follow that command by `docker rm $CONTAINER_NAME` if you don’t intend to restart the container as-is (with `docker start $CONTAINER_NAME`), in which case you need to re-use the `docker run ...` instructions from the beginning of this section to start a new container for your NIM.\
\
If you didn’t start a container with `--name`, examine the output of the `docker ps` command to get a container ID for the given image you used.\
\
## Kubernetes Installation [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#kubernetes-installation "Link to this heading")\
\
The [`nim-deploy` GitHub repository](https://github.com/NVIDIA/nim-deploy/tree/main?tab=readme-ov-file#introduction) showcases several reference implementations for Kubernetes installations. These examples are experimental, and might require modification for you to run in your particular cluster set-up.\
\
## Serving Models from Local Assets [\#](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/getting-started.html\#serving-models-from-local-assets "Link to this heading")\
\
NIM LLM Version 1.0.3 or later\
\
NIM for LLMs provides utilities which enable downloading models to a local directory either as a model repository or to NIM cache.\
See the [Utilities](https://docs.nvidia.com/nim/large-language-models/1.0.0/utilities.html) section for details.\
\
From a NIM container you can view and download models locally.\
\
1. Use the following commands to launch a NIM container for the model you want to deploy.\
\
\
\
\
\
```\
export NIM_IMAGE=nvcr.io/nim/meta/llama3-8b-instruct:1.0.0\
export NIM_CACHE_PATH=/path/to/cache\
mkdir -p "$NIM_CACHE_PATH"\
docker run -it --rm --name=llama3-8b-instruct \\
       -e LOG_LEVEL=$LOG_LEVEL \\
       -v $NIM_CACHE_PATH:/opt/nim/.cache \\
       $NIM_IMAGE \\
       bash -i\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
2. Use the `list-model-profiles` command to list the available profiles.\
\
\
\
\
\
```\
list-model-profiles\
#SYSTEM INFO\
#- Free GPUs:\
#  -  [26b3:10de] (0) NVIDIA RTX 5880 Ada Generation (RTX A6000 Ada) [current utilization: 1%]\
#  -  [26b3:10de] (1) NVIDIA RTX 5880 Ada Generation (RTX A6000 Ada) [current utilization: 1%]\
#  -  [1d01:10de] (2) NVIDIA GeForce GT 1030 [current utilization: 2%]\
#MODEL PROFILES\
#- Compatible with system and runnable:\
#  - 19031a45cf096b683c4d66fff2a072c0e164a24f19728a58771ebfc4c9ade44f (vllm-fp16-tp2)\
#  - 8835c31752fbc67ef658b20a9f78e056914fdef0660206d82f252d62fd96064d (vllm-fp16-tp1)\
#  - With LoRA support:\
#    - c5ffce8f82de1ce607df62a4b983e29347908fb9274a0b7a24537d6ff8390eb9 (vllm-fp16-tp2-lora)\
#    - 8d3824f766182a754159e88ad5a0bd465b1b4cf69ecf80bd6d6833753e945740 (vllm-fp16-tp1-lora)\
#- Incompatible with system:\
#  - dcd85d5e877e954f26c4a7248cd3b98c489fbde5f1cf68b4af11d665fa55778e (tensorrt_llm-h100-fp8-tp2-latency)\
#  - f59d52b0715ee1ecf01e6759dea23655b93ed26b12e57126d9ec43b397ea2b87 (tensorrt_llm-l40s-fp8-tp2-latency)\
#  - 30b562864b5b1e3b236f7b6d6a0998efbed491e4917323d04590f715aa9897dc (tensorrt_llm-h100-fp8-tp1-throughput)\
#  - 09e2f8e68f78ce94bf79d15b40a21333cea5d09dbe01ede63f6c957f4fcfab7b (tensorrt_llm-l40s-fp8-tp1-throughput)\
#  - a93a1a6b72643f2b2ee5e80ef25904f4d3f942a87f8d32da9e617eeccfaae04c (tensorrt_llm-a100-fp16-tp2-latency)\
#  - e0f4a47844733eb57f9f9c3566432acb8d20482a1d06ec1c0d71ece448e21086 (tensorrt_llm-a10g-fp16-tp2-latency)\
#  - 879b05541189ce8f6323656b25b7dff1930faca2abe552431848e62b7e767080 (tensorrt_llm-h100-fp16-tp2-latency)\
#  - 24199f79a562b187c52e644489177b6a4eae0c9fdad6f7d0a8cb3677f5b1bc89 (tensorrt_llm-l40s-fp16-tp2-latency)\
#  - 751382df4272eafc83f541f364d61b35aed9cce8c7b0c869269cea5a366cd08c (tensorrt_llm-a100-fp16-tp1-throughput)\
#  - c334b76d50783655bdf62b8138511456f7b23083553d310268d0d05f254c012b (tensorrt_llm-a10g-fp16-tp1-throughput)\
#  - cb52cbc73a6a71392094380f920a3548f27c5fcc9dab02a98dc1bcb3be9cf8d1 (tensorrt_llm-h100-fp16-tp1-throughput)\
#  - d8dd8af82e0035d7ca50b994d85a3740dbd84ddb4ed330e30c509e041ba79f80 (tensorrt_llm-l40s-fp16-tp1-throughput)\
#  - 9137f4d51dadb93c6b5864a19fd7c035bf0b718f3e15ae9474233ebd6468c359 (tensorrt_llm-a10g-fp16-tp2-throughput-lora)\
#  - cce57ae50c3af15625c1668d5ac4ccbe82f40fa2e8379cc7b842cc6c976fd334 (tensorrt_llm-a100-fp16-tp1-throughput-lora)\
#  - 3bdf6456ff21c19d5c7cc37010790448a4be613a1fd12916655dfab5a0dd9b8e (tensorrt_llm-h100-fp16-tp1-throughput-lora)\
#  - 388140213ee9615e643bda09d85082a21f51622c07bde3d0811d7c6998873a0b (tensorrt_llm-l40s-fp16-tp1-throughput-lora)\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
\
You can download any of these profiles to the NIM cache using the `download-to-cache` command. The following example downloads the `tensorrt_llm-l40s-fp8-tp1-throughput` profile to the NIM cache.\
\
```\
download-to-cache --profile 09e2f8e68f78ce94bf79d15b40a21333cea5d09dbe01ede63f6c957f4fcfab7b\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
You can also let the `download-to-cache` decide the most optimal profile given the hardware to download by providing no profiles to download, as shown in the following example.\
\
```\
download-to-cache\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
Further information on `download-to-cache` tool:\
\
```\
download-to-cache -h\
# Downloads selected or default model profiles to NIM cache. Can be used to pre-\
# cache profiles prior to deployment.\
\
# options:\
#   -h, --help            show this help message and exit\
#   --profiles [PROFILES ...], -p [PROFILES ...]\
#                         Profile hashes to download. If none are provided, the\
#                         optimal profile is downloaded. Multiple profiles can\
#                         be specified separated by spaces.\
#   --all                 Set this to download all profiles to cache\
#   --lora                Set this to download default lora profile. This\
#                         expects --profiles and --all arguments are not\
#                         specified.\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
Once you have downloaded a profile, use the `create-model-store` command to create a repository for a single model, as shown in the following example.\
\
```\
create-model-store --profile 09e2f8e68f78ce94bf79d15b40a21333cea5d09dbe01ede63f6c957f4fcfab7b --model-store /path/to/model-repository\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
NIM supports serving models in an Air Gap system (also known as air wall, air-gapping or disconnected network).\
If NIM detects a previously loaded profile in the cache, it serves that profile from the cache.\
After downloading the profiles to cache using `download-to-cache`, the cache can be transferred to an air-gapped system to run a NIM without any internet connection or with no connection to the NGC registry.\
\
Too see this in action, don’t provide the NGC\_API\_KEY, as shown in the following example.\
\
```\
# Choose a container name for bookkeeping\
export CONTAINER_NAME=Llama3-8B-Instruct\
\
# The container name from the previous ngc registgry image list command\
Repository=nim/meta/llama3-8b-instruct\
Latest_Tag=1.0.0\
\
# Choose a LLM NIM Image from NGC\
export IMG_NAME="${__ngc_container_root}/${Repository}:${Latest_Tag}"\
\
# Choose a path on your system to cache the downloaded models\
export LOCAL_NIM_CACHE=~/.cache/nim\
mkdir -p "$LOCAL_NIM_CACHE"\
\
export MODEL_REPO=/path/to/model-repository\
export NIM_SERVED_MODEL_NAME=my-model\
\
# Assuming the command run prior was `download-to-cache`, downloading the optimal profile\
\
docker run -it --rm --name=$CONTAINER_NAME \\
  --runtime=nvidia \\
  --gpus all \\
  --shm-size=16GB \\
  -v "$LOCAL_NIM_CACHE:/opt/nim/.cache" \\
  -u $(id -u) \\
  -p 8000:8000 \\
  $IMG_NAME\
\
# Assuming the command run prior was `download-to-cache --profile 09e2f8e68f78ce94bf79d15b40a21333cea5d09dbe01ede63f6c957f4fcfab7b`\
\
docker run -it --rm --name=$CONTAINER_NAME \\
  --runtime=nvidia \\
  --gpus all \\
  --shm-size=16GB \\
  -e NIM_MODEL_PROFILE=09e2f8e68f78ce94bf79d15b40a21333cea5d09dbe01ede63f6c957f4fcfab7b \\
  -v "$LOCAL_NIM_CACHE:/opt/nim/.cache" \\
  -u $(id -u) \\
  -p 8000:8000 \\
  $IMG_NAME\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
Another option for the air gap route is to deploy the model repository created using the `create-model-store` command, as shown in the following example.\
\
```\
# Choose a container name for bookkeeping\
export CONTAINER_NAME=Llama3-8B-Instruct\
\
# The container name from the previous ngc registgry image list command\
Repository=nim/meta/llama3-8b-instruct\
Latest_Tag=1.0.0\
\
# Choose a LLM NIM Image from NGC\
export IMG_NAME="${__ngc_container_root}/${Repository}:${Latest_Tag}"\
\
# Choose a path on your system to cache the downloaded models\
export LOCAL_NIM_CACHE=~/.cache/nim\
mkdir -p "$LOCAL_NIM_CACHE"\
\
export MODEL_REPO=/path/to/model-repository\
export NIM_SERVED_MODEL_NAME=my-model\
\
docker run -it --rm --name=$CONTAINER_NAME \\
  --runtime=nvidia \\
  --gpus all \\
  --shm-size=16GB \\
  -e NIM_MODEL_NAME=/model-repo \\
  -e NIM_SERVED_MODEL_NAME \\
  -v $MODEL_REPO:/model-repo \\
  -u $(id -u) \\
  -p 8000:8000 \\
  $IMG_NAME\
```\
\
Copy to clipboard\
\
You can also run a docker container and transport a ready cache from a connected to an air gap system or a model repository.\
\

# Automating NVIDIA SDK Manager Setup on WSL2

[![Ttbenjamin](https://miro.medium.com/v2/da:true/resize:fill:32:32/0*0JvGv4QfUojTNTv5)](https://medium.com/@ttbenjamin12345?source=post_page---byline--94cca93581f6---------------------------------------)

[Ttbenjamin](https://medium.com/@ttbenjamin12345?source=post_page---byline--94cca93581f6---------------------------------------)

Follow

7 min read

·

Jan 28, 2025

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3D94cca93581f6&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2F%40ttbenjamin12345%2Fautomating-nvidia-sdk-manager-setup-on-wsl2-94cca93581f6&source=---header_actions--94cca93581f6---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

When I started working on a PowerShell script to automate the setup of NVIDIA SDK Manager on the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL), I expected challenges, but the depth of troubleshooting and problem-solving required was more than I anticipated (I definitely re-filled my coffee cup more times than I can count). This project involved more than just writing scripts; it was about overcoming real software development hurdles like managing dependencies, ensuring compatibility across systems, and making everything user-friendly (I now sympathize with puzzle designers everywhere). This blog post will walk you through my journey of developing this tool. I’ll share the technical struggles, including the issues with converting PowerShell scripts into executable files, the efforts to reduce or eliminate pesky red “ERROR” lines, and the moment I realized I might need a second pot of coffee. Whether you’re an experienced developer or just starting, I hope my experiences can provide some practical insights and help you navigate your projects more effectively.

## Getting Started

The project kicked off with a pretty straightforward objective: to automate the setup of the Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) along with the NVIDIA SDK Manager on Windows machines. The core idea was to streamline the often cumbersome process of manually installing and configuring these tools, which are crucial for developing applications that leverage NVIDIA’s powerful GPU-accelerated libraries.

The PowerShell script I envisioned would not only install WSL and set it to its required version but also handle the download and installation of the NVIDIA SDK Manager. This included managing all necessary dependencies, such as wget and Google Chrome, which the SDK Manager required to function properly. The ultimate goal was to simplify the user’s experience from start to finish, ensuring that with a single execution of the script, all components would be set up, configured, and ready to use.

Here’s what the script was supposed to do:

- Detect and set up the user’s home and download paths.
- Check if the latest version of the SDK Manager .deb file was in the Downloads directory and, if not, prompt the user to download it.
- Install WSL if it wasn’t already installed, then download and install Ubuntu 22.04 LTS.
- Automatically handle the installation of Ubuntu, including setting up a new UNIX username and password.
- Download and install essential tools and libraries, preparing the system for the SDK Manager.
- Launch the SDK Manager and ensure all its components were properly configured.

**SURELY, it couldn’t be that hard. Right?** (Famous last words before I realized I’d be debugging bright red errors at 2 AM.)

## The Development Process: Crafting the Script

The journey of developing the PowerShell script to automate the setup of WSL, install Google Chrome, mount a Windows directory, and install the NVIDIA SDK Manager was riddled with all sorts of unexpected technical challenges. (If debugging is an art, then I’m probably Picasso by now.) Here’s a detailed look:

## Initial Setup and WSL Configuration

**Ensuring WSL Version 2** The first step was to ensure the script set the default WSL version to 2. This was crucial for compatibility with Ubuntu 22.04 and the NVIDIA SDK Manager.

## Requiring Multiple Password Entries

**Sudo Commands Prompting for Password** The script required multiple password entries for sudo commands, which disrupted the flow. I had to look for ways to group sudo commands or find methods to minimize password prompts to streamline the process. (I suspect my keyboard now has a dedicated “sudo password” key.)

## Restarting Bash with Each Command

**Maintaining a Single WSL Session** Using wsl -d multiple times caused the WSL bash to restart with each command, disrupting the script flow. To address this, I combined all necessary post-initial setup commands into a single bash -c command to maintain a continuous WSL session.

## Automating Google Chrome Installation

**Download and Install Commands** Automating the installation of Google Chrome in WSL required ensuring the proper execution of download and installation commands. Handling wget and dpkg commands within the script was necessary. (I also discovered how to quiet the “ERROR” lines from wget by adding -q — a big plus if you hate walls of red text.)

## Mounting Windows Directory in WSL

**Path Recognition Issues** Mounting the Windows directory in WSL presented problems with path recognition. Correcting the path to use proper escape sequences and ensuring the directory existed before attempting to mount was essential.

## Finalizing SDK Manager Installation and Execution

**Automating SDK Manager Installation** Ensuring the SDK Manager was correctly placed in the mounted directory and installed without manual intervention was a significant challenge. The script needed to handle the opening and running of the SDK Manager to guide users through necessary steps seamlessly.

## Script Execution Errors and Debugging

**Handling Command Errors** Encountering and resolving issues like “command not found,” mounting errors, and failed installations required thorough debugging. Ensuring all commands ran smoothly in one session without requiring script re-runs or manual restarts was critical.

## Get Ttbenjamin’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

Subscribe

Remember me for faster sign in

**Handling Script Execution in PowerShell** Configuring PowerShell to allow script execution involved dealing with execution policies. Ensuring the script could handle credential prompts and properly pass credentials to WSL was necessary for a seamless experience. (I would later implement this in a batch file and still curse at red error text until I discovered -q mode for wget!)

## Final Adjustments and Testing

**End-to-End Automation** Making necessary adjustments based on feedback was key to ensuring the script ran end-to-end without user interruptions. The final script aimed to automatically open and set up the WSL environment, install required software (including Chrome with fewer bright red lines, thanks to -q), and launch the SDK Manager seamlessly.

## Why a “Prerequisite Script”?

During testing, I realized some users had never installed WSL at all, while others had partial or older setups. To avoid forcing everyone through a total reset, I split things into two steps:

1. **A prerequisite script** that checks and enables WSL features, installs kernel updates if needed, and sets up execution policies. It basically ensures any machine is ready for WSL.
2. **The main installer** that imports the custom Ubuntu tar and sets up NVIDIA SDK Manager.

This approach keeps things simple and prevents messing with users’ existing distributions if they already have a functioning WSL environment.

## Overcoming the Hard Stuff

## Executable Conversion Issues

The first major roadblock was the conversion of the PowerShell script into an executable file. This process was intended to simplify distribution and usage but introduced unexpected complexities. For instance, the executable would often fail to display outputs properly, leaving users blind to the script’s progress and prompts (a stealth script, if you will).

## Dependency Management Struggles

Dependency management posed another significant challenge — particularly with controlling package installations across different user environments. Unmet dependencies frequently triggered error messages until I hammered out a better approach (which included setting DEBIAN\_FRONTEND=noninteractive to avoid more of those red lines than I get on a busy highway).

## The GUI Experiment

To address some of these issues, I experimented with creating a graphical user interface (GUI) for the script (because we always assume a fancy progress bar will solve everything). However, this came with challenges of its own, like ensuring the GUI worked consistently on various Windows versions. In the end, I decided to skip a fully integrated GUI for simplicity’s sake.

## The C\# Detour: From PowerShell to Visual Studio

After encountering several limitations with the PowerShell script, especially around the executable conversion, I decided to rewrite the script in C#. If “exe” didn’t work, surely “C#” was the next best thing (the logic of the tired developer at 3 AM).

### Choosing C\#

- **Stability**: C# applications are known for their stability within the .NET framework.
- **IDE Support**: Visual Studio provided extensive tools for debugging and testing.
- **Rich Libraries**: C# offered robust libraries for file operations and UI elements.

### Learning Curve and Integration Challenges

The shift to C# wasn’t easy. Some functionalities straightforward in PowerShell (like seamlessly calling wsl) required more convoluted approaches in C#. On the bright side, I developed a deeper respect for object-oriented paradigms (and for people who do C# UIs full-time).

### Reflections

Ultimately, the C# rewrite provided mixed results, revealing how critical it is to pick the right tool for the job. (At least my red “ERROR” lines had different shapes in C#.)

## My Personal “Aha” Moments

I tackled the bright red lines in the console by:

1. **Quieting Wget’s Progress** Adding -q to wget cut down on giant error-laden progress indicators.
2. **Setting APT to Non-Interactive** export DEBIAN\_FRONTEND=noninteractive minimized scary “Dialog frontend” warnings. (No more monstrous multi-line errors telling me my screen wasn’t tall enough for apt’s tastes.)

These two changes saved me a boatload of confusion (and no small measure of hair-pulling) when trudging through logs.

## Practical Bits

- **Batch files** simplified script calling and credential handling.
- **Logging** everything helped identify which commands were causing trouble.
- **Documentation** (plus plenty of coffee) ensured I remembered each step.

## Making It User-Friendly

One of my primary goals was to ensure the software was not only powerful but also accessible to users with various levels of technical expertise (including that tech-savvy aunt who somehow still uses dial-up).

## Streamlining with Batch Files

Batch files allowed a single-click user experience, sidestepping complicated PowerShell execution policies and letting me keep the distribution of the tool as straightforward as possible.

## Iterative Development

I relied on feedback from users and my own debugging attempts to polish the final product. Each little tweak (like the -q for wget or the batch file approach) made the script more robust and user-friendly.

## Comprehensive Documentation

I wrote step-by-step guides in as plain language as I could manage (although some jargon is inevitable), aiming for a smooth onboarding process.

## Wrapping Up: A Few Final Thoughts

Developing an automated WSL + NVIDIA SDK Manager installer led me through debugging marathons, detours into C#, and the revelation that a minor manual login step is not the end of the world. Here are my main takeaways:

- **Flexibility Is Key**: Switching among PowerShell, batch files, or C# taught me when to pivot.
- **Persistence Pays Off**: Each time I tackled one batch of bright red errors, another batch turned up — but eventually, we made it.
- **Community Helps**: Whether it’s a post on a forum or someone’s comment on GitHub, you often find exactly what you need in the experiences of others.

I encourage you to try the script, enjoy the (mostly) quiet logs (thanks to -q), and let me know if you spot any new challenges. I’ll keep coffee on standby for those late-night debugging sessions.

Have a look at my lil ol’ script below:

[Ternatsu2/NVIDIA-SDK-WSL-Installer: An automated installer for WSL2, a custom Ubuntu 22.04 distro, and NVIDIA SDK Manager. It streamlines setup, handling WSL installation, dependencies, and configuration for a hassle-free NVIDIA development experience on Windows.](https://github.com/Ternatsu2/NVIDIA-SDK-WSL-Installer)

**Thanks for reading!** Feel free to share feedback, suggestions, or your own war stories with red lines in the console. Let’s continue pushing the boundaries of automation — while hopefully seeing fewer bright red lines in our terminals!

# Enable NVIDIA CUDA on WSL

Feedback

Summarize this article for me


Windows 11 and later updates of Windows 10 support running existing ML tools, libraries, and popular frameworks that use NVIDIA CUDA for GPU hardware acceleration inside a Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) instance. This includes PyTorch and TensorFlow as well as all the Docker and NVIDIA Container Toolkit support available in a native Linux environment.

[Section titled: Install Windows 11 or Windows 10, version 21H2](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/ai/directml/gpu-cuda-in-wsl#install-windows-11-or-windows-10-version-21h2)

## Install Windows 11 or Windows 10, version 21H2

To use these features, you can download and install [Windows 11](https://microsoft.com/software-download/windows11) or [Windows 10, version 21H2](https://microsoft.com/software-download/windows10).

[Section titled: Install the GPU driver](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/ai/directml/gpu-cuda-in-wsl#install-the-gpu-driver)

## Install the GPU driver

Download and install the [NVIDIA CUDA enabled driver for WSL](https://www.nvidia.com/download/index.aspx) to use with your existing CUDA ML workflows. For more info about which driver to install, see:

- [Getting Started with CUDA on WSL 2](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/wsl-user-guide/index.html#getting-started-with-cuda-on-wsl)
- [CUDA on Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL)](https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda/wsl)

[Section titled: Install WSL](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/ai/directml/gpu-cuda-in-wsl#install-wsl)

## Install WSL

Once you've installed the above driver, ensure you [enable WSL](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install-win10) and [install a glibc-based distribution](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install-win10#install-your-linux-distribution-of-choice), such as Ubuntu or Debian. Ensure you have the latest kernel by selecting **Check for updates** in the **Windows Update** section of Windows Settings.

Note

Ensure you have **Receive updates for other Microsoft products** enabled. You can find it in **Advanced options** within the **Windows Update** section of Windows Settings.

For these features, you need a kernel version of 5.10.43.3 or higher. You can check the version number by running the following command in PowerShell.

PowerShell


Copy

```powershell
wsl cat /proc/version
```

[Section titled: Get started with NVIDIA CUDA](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/ai/directml/gpu-cuda-in-wsl#get-started-with-nvidia-cuda)

## Get started with NVIDIA CUDA

Now follow the instructions in the [NVIDIA CUDA on WSL User Guide](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/wsl-user-guide/index.html#getting-started-with-cuda-on-wsl-2) and you can start using your exisiting Linux workflows through [NVIDIA Docker](https://github.com/NVIDIA/nvidia-docker), or by installing [PyTorch](https://pytorch.org/get-started/locally/) or [TensorFlow](https://www.tensorflow.org/install/gpu) inside WSL.

Share feedback on NVIDIA's support via their [Community forum for CUDA on WSL](https://forums.developer.nvidia.com/c/accelerated-computing/cuda/cuda-on-windows-subsystem-for-linux-wsl-2/303).
